body.conclusion: Ace Alliance finds the proposed "Cardano Vision 2026: Human Centred, Scalable, Post Quantum Secure - IO Research" Treasury Withdrawal Governance Action Constitutional.
body.counterargumentDiscussion:
A reasonable counterargument arises from Article II.7.1 and concerns the proposal's research-to-deployment translation. The proposal acknowledges that IO Research has produced more than 250 peer-reviewed papers since its inception, of which approximately 20% have been implemented in Cardano, and proposes a new integrated IOR, ARC, and Cardano Business Unit operating model intended to improve that conversion rate. The proposal does not specify how research teams interact with engineering teams that productionize the work, who has decision authority at each handoff, what happens when engineering rejects or deprioritizes a research output, or how implementation responsibility is assigned across the pipeline. On the strongest reading, where roughly four-fifths of the proposer's historic output has not landed on Cardano, a TWGA of this size arguably leaves the community uncertain whether the funded work will move the Cardano blockchain ecosystem forward in proportion to the spend. We acknowledge that this argument has force.

We reject that view on these facts. Academic and foundational research operates on a different scope than direct on-chain implementation: the constitutional purpose of a research-funding TWGA can include knowledge production, peer-reviewed publication, and theoretical groundwork that strengthens Cardano's standing in the broader cryptographic and distributed-systems community, even where a portion of that output does not enter mainnet code paths. Whether the appropriate breadth of research scope is wider or narrower than what this proposal sets is a question for DReps, who decide whether to accept the scope of work the proposer has chosen to put forward. The specification floor of Article II.7.1 is met on the record, and Article II.7.5 places Administrator authority with Intersect through the TRSC and PSSC framework. The handoff-authority and engineering-rejection questions are organizational design choices, not constitutional defects.

A second counterargument arises from the costs-and-expenses requirement of Article II.7.1 and concerns the proposal's level of overhead disclosure. The proposal discloses aggregate dollar splits at the IOR ($4.2M / 19.5 FTE) and ARC ($3.7M / 17.5 FTE) level and an inclusive per-FTE rate of approximately $219.5k, but it does not break out university administrative fees, subcontracting-rate disclosure, or the negotiation history behind those overheads. Where a TWGA of this size routes a material portion of its spend through academic partners, more transparent overhead disclosure would let DReps better assess what fraction of the withdrawal pays for direct research labor versus institutional overhead. We acknowledge that this argument has force, and that overhead disclosure on this proposal could have been done better.

We reject that view on these facts. Article II.7.1 requires specification of "the relevant costs and expenses of the proposed activities" and not a particular overhead-disclosure schema, fee-line breakout, or negotiation transcript. The proposal supplies costs at the level the provision sets, and the Administrator under Article II.7.5 is the constitutional enforcement layer for contractual obligations: Intersect, acting on behalf of Cardano Development Holdings, executes a Legal Contract that sets out milestone acceptance, payment amounts, and delivery dates, with a 3rd Party Assurer signing off against the agreed deliverables and the standard refund mechanism, which returns unspent funds to the Treasury on non-disbursement and proportionally on cancelled or reduced scope. Whether the level of upfront contract and overhead information is sufficient for DReps to vote with confidence is a question for DReps, not a defect under Article II.7.1.

A third counterargument concerns alignment between the proposed research program and the Cardano 2030 Vision and Strategy that the community recently affirmed through an Info Action poll of DReps. The community's expression of its 2030 priorities, having been put to and supported in an on-chain vote, carries real force as a signal of where the community wants Cardano's strategic direction to go. The proposal does not articulate, with particularity, how IO Research's seven work packages map to those declared community values and strategies, leaving DReps without an explicit fit narrative they can use to test the spend against the community's own expressed priorities. We acknowledge that this argument has force.

We reject that view on these facts. Article III.1.4 confines the Constitutional Committee to voting on "the constitutionality of governance actions," which means that strategic-fit between this TWGA and a community-expressed strategy, however forceful that expression, is not within the Committee's scope of review. The Info Action that articulated the 2030 priorities is non-binding by design and does not create a constitutional precondition that subsequent Treasury Withdrawals align with its contents. Whether this proposal is complementary to previously declared community values and strategies is a DRep judgment, not a constitutional defect, and DReps remain the proper venue for testing that fit when they vote.
body.rationaleStatement:
A [PDF version][pdf-link] of this rationale is also made available.

[pdf-link]: ipfs://QmTkGYENnn3HrEFD9NKMiSgJDHagyampgPksuqrr2DBXCt

"Cardano Vision 2026: Human Centred, Scalable, Post Quantum Secure - IO Research" (gov_action1ttgs45ulfxs0jwkfrecystc3flduhszmyzk8wnd7yw5za77tsg9qq4afmus) is a Treasury Withdrawals Governance Action and is therefore governed by both the general proposal standards in Article II, Section 6 and the additional Treasury Withdrawal standards in Article II, Section 7 of the Cardano Constitution.

This TWGA seeks to withdraw ₳32,916,667 from the Cardano Treasury to fund Input Output Research's 2026 program, delivering an integrated research-to-deployment pipeline across IOR (scientific research), ARC (applied engineering), and the Cardano Business Unit (product alignment). The program produces 42 outputs across the innovation pipeline (approximately 38 papers and technical reports, 8 Cardano Problem Statements and 12 prototypes, and 5 Cardano Improvement Proposals), organized into seven work packages spanning trust and security infrastructure, scalability and execution, developer platform and user experience, applications and liquidity, economic systems and incentives, governance and identity, and delivery and dissemination. Delivery runs across the 2026 program year, with the work carried out by a 9-partner consortium led by Input Output and including leading academic institutions and Eryx. Funds are directed into Intersect's 2025 Treasury Reserve Smart Contract (stake17xzc8pt7fgf0lc0x7eq6z7z6puhsxmzktna7dluahrj6g6ghh5qjr), with subsequent migration to a 2026 TRSC once established, and the withdrawal is administered by Intersect on behalf of Cardano Development Holdings.

The procedural standards of Article II.6 are satisfied: the proposal anchors to an IPFS-hosted document with the on-chain blake2b-256 hash, a content-addressed form "immutable and incapable of being altered after submission," and the metadata provides the title, abstract, justification, and supporting materials required by II.6.2.

The substantive Article II.7 requirements are met as well. Under Article II.7.1, "Treasury Withdrawals actions must specify the terms of the withdrawal," and the proposal specifies the purpose (a research-to-deployment pipeline across seven work packages and 42 deliverables), period (the 2026 program year with quarterly delivery updates and bi-annual reports via Intersect), itemized costs (₳32,916,667 across 36 FTEs at an average inclusive rate of approximately $219.5k per FTE, with a per-work-package FTE allocation table), and refund circumstances (end-of-period sweep and proportional return on cancelled or reduced deliverables). The disclosure requirement of Article II.7.2 is met by a Prior Treasury Receipts table identifying ₳130,708,860 allocated and ₳78,459,777 withdrawn across IO workstreams, with each prior allocation tied to its on-chain governance action hash. The 350M Net Change Limit covering Epoch 613 to Epoch 713 is set, and this ₳32,916,667 ask does not exceed it, satisfying Article II.7.3 and Appendix I guardrail TREASURY-02a.

A 3rd Party Assurer funded as part of the withdrawal supports milestone acceptance against the deliverables detailed in the Legal Contract between Input Output and Cardano Development Holdings, satisfying the periodic independent audit and oversight allocation under Article II.7.4. Article II.7.5 is satisfied by Intersect's designation as Administrator, acting on behalf of Cardano Development Holdings, operating through the TRSC and PSSC framework with milestone-based disbursement controls and a five-member external Oversight Committee (Sundae Labs, Cardano Foundation, Dquadrant, Xerberus, NMKR). The custody requirement of Article II.7.6 is satisfied by the proposal's commitment that TRSC and PSSC instances cannot be staked with an SPO and will be delegated to the auto-abstain predefined DRep, with a community-audit dashboard, on the same Intersect TRSC pattern we have accepted as constitutional in prior actions in this family.
body.summary: Ace Alliance finds the proposed "Cardano Vision 2026: Human Centred, Scalable, Post Quantum Secure - IO Research" Treasury Withdrawals Governance Action constitutional. Rationales are archived at https://github.com/ace-alliance/ace-voting/
